ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Japanese-style fried chicken salad; closest match is the generic chicken salad food product class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken, fried — used as the dominant component anchor; salad composite not separately coded in FoodEx2 at this specificity level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for fried chicken salad; A0CKM (fried chicken) used as the primary ingredient anchor. Salad vegetable components are secondary.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried chicken salad as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried chicken salad weighing approximately 350 g, comprising crispy deep-fried chicken pieces over a bed of mixed salad vegetables dressed with a Japanese-style dressing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (FDC ID 171477) combined with USDA FoodData Central — Salad, vegetable, tossed, without dressing (FDC ID 169977); composite estimate weighted approximately 45% fried chicken, 40% mixed salad vegetables, 15% Japanese sesame dressing.
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Japanese-style fried chicken salad as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FDC entries for batter-fried chicken (FDC ID 171477) and tossed vegetable salad (FDC ID 169977), with a minor contribution from a sesame-soy dressing estimate. The input record's per-100g values (energy 165.7 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 10.9 g, carbohydrate 7.1 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with cross-validation against the USDA composite. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed salad with fried protein component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese fried chicken salad. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed salad with protein (GI ~40–50 range). The low GI reflects the dominant vegetable base, moderate protein, and fat content which slow gastric emptying. GL per serving = (45 × 7.1 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350 g serving) / 100 / 100 = approximately 11.2 g. Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) used as the peer-reviewed GI reference framework.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 271 mg per 100 g (949 mg per 350 g serving) approaches the 1000 mg per meal threshold for hypertensive individuals, with additional sodium risk from soy-based Japanese dressings.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request dressing on the side and choose reduced-sodium soy dressing options to keep total sodium per meal below 800 mg; the vegetable base provides potassium that partially offsets sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.1 g per 100 g) and estimated low GI (~45) combined with 10 g protein per 100 g support glycaemic stability; dietary fibre (1.1 g per 100 g) from salad vegetables further slows glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in a balanced meal plan; they should request dressing on the side to avoid hidden sugars in sweet sesame or teriyaki-style dressings, and avoid adding croutons or sweet corn which raise the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying introduces saturated fat (2.3 g per 100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g per 100 g), and the sodium level (271 mg per 100 g) is borderline elevated; the vegetable base provides antioxidants and vitamin C (4.3 mg per 100 g) which are cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, request grilled chicken as a substitute for fried chicken to eliminate trans fat and reduce saturated fat, and choose a light vinaigrette dressing over creamy or soy-heavy options.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; this is a hard disqualifier for RA dietary suitability despite the anti-inflammatory vegetable components.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute grilled or baked chicken for the fried component to eliminate AGE formation; tomato in the salad is a nightshade and may trigger individual RA flares and should be requested omitted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken Salad (炸鸡沙拉; フライドチキンサラダ; Salad Ayam Goreng) is a Japanese-influenced salad dish served in Singapore's Japanese curry restaurant segment. It features crispy deep-fried chicken pieces — typically karaage-style (soy-marinated, potato-starch-coated) or katsu-style (panko-breaded) — placed over a bed of fresh mixed salad vegetables that commonly include shredded cabbage, carrot, cucumber, cherry tomato, and corn, dressed with a Japanese sesame or wafu (Japanese-style) dressing. The chicken provides a satisfying crunch and savoury umami flavour, while the vegetables contribute freshness, crispness, and a mild sweetness. The dish is moderate in calories (~166 kcal per 100 g; ~580 kcal per 350 g plate), low in carbohydrates (7.1 g per 100 g), and provides a meaningful protein contribution (10 g per 100 g) from the chicken. Sodium is borderline elevated at 271 mg per 100 g, largely from the dressing and chicken marinade. The estimated glycaemic index is low (~45), making it broadly suitable for blood sugar management when dressing is controlled. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management and is conditionally suitable for hypertension with dressing modification, but is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory AGEs generated by deep-frying. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and contains common allergens including gluten, soy, egg, and sesame.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried chicken salad?
Is fried chicken salad good for diabetics?
Can I eat fried chicken salad if I have high blood pressure?
Is fried chicken salad suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Japanese-style fried chicken salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for batter-fried chicken and tossed vegetable salad, cross-validated against the input record's grabfood-sourced values. The input values are broadly consistent with the USDA composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite fried chicken salad dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central at the composite level; individual component data exists but reliable weighted composite fatty acid data is not available as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central at the composite level as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Japanese fried chicken salad dish are not available at the composite level in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 4. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FDC entries (FDC ID 171477 batter-fried chicken; FDC ID 169977 tossed vegetable salad) and are consistent with the input record's grabfood-sourced values which have been retained. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was assessed but does not constitute an approved primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved source. 6. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. This dish is Japanese cuisine served in Singapore; both Japanese (G6 Japanese cuisine rule) and Singaporean multilingual coverage (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi — G6 Singaporean dish rule) have been applied as the country_of_origin is Singapore.
